content:
# Acts Chapter 9 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 235-237 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/ACT_009_page_001.jpg, ACT_009_page_002.jpg, ACT_009_page_003.jpg

---

**1** But Saul, still breathing threat and murder against the disciples of the Lord, went to the high priest **2** and asked from him letters to Damascus, to the synagogues, so that if he found any who were of the Way, both men and women, he might bring them bound to Jerusalem. **3** And as he journeyed, it came to pass that he drew near to Damascus, and suddenly a light from heaven flashed around him. **4** And falling to the ground, he heard a voice saying to him, "Saul, Saul, why do you persecute me?" **5** And he said, "Who are you, Lord?" And he said, "I am Jesus, whom you are persecuting. **6** But rise and enter into the city, and it shall be told to you what you must do." **7** And the men who were journeying with him stood speechless, hearing indeed the voice but seeing no one. **8** And Saul arose from the ground, and though his eyes were opened, he saw nothing. And leading him by the hand, they brought him into Damascus. **9** And he was three days without sight, and neither ate nor drank.

**10** Now there was a certain disciple in Damascus named Ananias, and the Lord said to him in a vision, "Ananias." And he said, "Behold, I am here, Lord." **11** And the Lord said to him, "Rise and go to the street called Straight, and inquire at the house of Judas for one named Saul, of Tarsus; for behold, he is praying, **12** and he has seen in a vision a man named Ananias coming in and laying hands on him, so that he might receive his sight." **13** But Ananias answered, "Lord, I have heard from many concerning this man, how much evil he has done to your saints in Jerusalem. **14** And here he has authority from the chief priests to bind all who call upon your name." **15** But the Lord said to him, "Go, for he is a chosen vessel to me, to bear my name before the Gentiles and kings and the sons of Israel. **16** For I will show him how much he must suffer for the sake of my name."

**17** And Ananias departed and entered the house, and laying his hands upon him, he said, "Brother Saul, the Lord has sent me — Jesus, who appeared to you on the road by which you came — so that you may receive your sight and be filled with the Holy Spirit." **18** And immediately there fell from his eyes something like scales, and he received his sight. And he rose and was baptized, **19** and having taken food, he was strengthened.

And he was with the disciples in Damascus for some days. **20** And immediately in the synagogues he proclaimed Jesus, that this one is the Son of God. **21** And all who heard were amazed and said, "Is this not the one who in Jerusalem destroyed those who call upon this name, and who had come here for this purpose, that he might bring them bound before the chief priests?" **22** But Saul increased all the more in strength and confounded the Jews dwelling in Damascus, proving that this is the Christ.

**23** And when a considerable number of days had been fulfilled, the Jews plotted together to kill him. **24** But their plot became known to Saul. And they were also watching the gates day and night, so that they might kill him. **25** But his disciples, taking him by night, let him down through the wall, lowering him in a basket.

**26** And arriving in Jerusalem, he attempted to join himself to the disciples, and they were all afraid of him, not believing that he was a disciple. **27** But Barnabas took hold of him and brought him to the apostles, and he declared to them how on the road he had seen the Lord, and that he had spoken to him, and how in Damascus he had spoken boldly in the name of Jesus. **28** And he was with them, going in and going out in Jerusalem, speaking boldly in the name of the Lord. **29** And he was speaking and disputing with the Hellenists, but they were attempting to kill him. **30** And when the brothers learned of this, they brought him down to Caesarea and sent him away to Tarsus.

**31** So the church throughout all Judea and Galilee and Samaria had peace, being built up and walking in the fear of the Lord and in the encouragement of the Holy Spirit, and it was multiplied.

**32** And it came to pass that Peter, passing through all the regions, came down also to the saints dwelling in Lydda. **33** And he found there a certain man named Aeneas, who for eight years had been lying on a mat, for he was paralyzed. **34** And Peter said to him, "Aeneas, Jesus the Christ heals you. Rise and make your bed." And immediately he rose. **35** And all those dwelling in Lydda and Sharon saw him, and they turned to the Lord.

**36** Now in Joppa there was a certain disciple named Tabitha, which translated is called Dorcas. She was full of good works and acts of charity which she was doing. **37** And it came to pass in those days that she became sick and died. And when they had washed her, they placed her in an upper room. **38** And since Lydda was near to Joppa, the disciples, having heard that Peter was in it, sent two men to him, urging, "Do not delay to come to us." **39** And Peter arose and went with them. And when he arrived, they brought him up to the upper room, and all the widows stood beside him weeping and showing the tunics and garments that Dorcas had made while she was with them. **40** But Peter, having put them all outside and having knelt down, prayed. And turning to the body, he said, "Tabitha, arise." And she opened her eyes, and seeing Peter, she sat up. **41** And giving her his hand, he raised her up. And calling the saints and the widows, he presented her alive. **42** And it became known throughout all Joppa, and many believed in the Lord. **43** And it came to pass that he remained in Joppa for a considerable number of days with a certain Simon, a tanner.

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

**Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus):**

Acts 9 begins in the fourth (rightmost) column of page 235 (folio 117v), continues across all four columns of page 236 (folio 118r), and concludes in the first column of page 237 (folio 118v). The text is written in Sinaiticus's characteristic four-column majuscule format without word spacing. Several corrections by later hands are visible, particularly in the Damascus road narrative.

**Key Translation Decisions:**

1. **"the Way" (v. 2):** Rendered from Greek *tes hodou*. This is a technical designation for the early Christian movement. Sinaiticus preserves this reading clearly. I have capitalized "Way" to reflect its use as a proper title.

2. **"breathing threat and murder" (v. 1):** The Sinaiticus text reads *empneon apeiles kai phonou* — literally "breathing of threat and of murder." The genitive construction conveys that threat and murder were the very atmosphere Saul exhaled. I have rendered this closely while allowing natural English phrasing.

3. **"chosen vessel" (v. 15):** Greek *skeuos ekloges* — literally "vessel of election/choosing." This is a Hebraic genitive of quality. "Chosen vessel" preserves the concrete metaphor of the Greek while conveying the adjectival force of the genitive.

4. **"something like scales" (v. 18):** Greek *hos lepides* — "as scales." The text uses a comparative particle, indicating a likeness rather than literal identification. The translation preserves this careful ambiguity.

5. **"the church" (v. 31):** Sinaiticus reads *he ekklesia* (singular) — "the church" rather than "the churches." This is a notable reading in Sinaiticus, presenting the early community as a single unified body across Judea, Galilee, and Samaria.

6. **"make your bed" (v. 34):** Greek *stroson seauto* — literally "spread for yourself." This refers to rolling up or arranging one's sleeping mat, a concrete demonstration of restored physical ability. I have rendered it idiomatically as "make your bed" to preserve clarity.

7. **"Tabitha / Dorcas" (v. 36):** The Aramaic name *Tabitha* is translated by Luke himself into its Greek equivalent *Dorkas* (Gazelle). I have preserved both names and Luke's own explanatory note, as this bilingual detail reflects the cultural setting of the early church.

8. **"acts of charity" (v. 36):** Greek *eleemosynon* — this word encompasses both the disposition of mercy and its concrete expression in almsgiving. "Acts of charity" captures both dimensions.

9. **Sinaiticus variant at v. 5-6:** Sinaiticus lacks the longer interpolation found in the Textus Receptus ("It is hard for you to kick against the goads... Lord, what will you have me do?"). The shorter reading is followed here as it reflects the manuscript being translated.

10. **"in the name of the Lord" (v. 28):** Sinaiticus reads *en to onomati tou kyriou* here, which some manuscripts render as "in the name of Jesus." I have followed the Sinaiticus reading.

-- LOGOS
